narrative: Dear Maria, imagine the admissions team looking at your application not just as a stack of numbers, but as the opening chapters of your story as a future lawyer—and unfortunately, the early signs painted a picture that made them worry about your chances of passing the bar exam down the line. Your first-year grades placed you in the bottom decile of your class, which stood out as the strongest signal, suggesting the rigorous demands of law school might hit harder than expected right from the start. That pattern held steady by your third year, still in the bottom decile, reinforcing the sense that keeping up with the competition could be an ongoing challenge. Your LSAT score of 30 added to those concerns, as it fell short of what typically predicts bar success in competitive programs. Even with your upper-middle family income, which might offer some support, it didn't outweigh the academic hurdles in the model's view. As a Hispanic woman studying full-time with an undergraduate GPA of 2.6, these details together tipped the scales toward a 68% predicted chance of bar failure, leading to the tough call to reject your application. It's not that your background or determination aren't assets—they absolutely are—but the model saw this specific combination as a risky bet for the bar. The good news? This isn't the end of your path; many students turn things around by targeting those early grades and test scores head-on. You could retake the LSAT, build a stronger undergrad record through post-bac courses, or seek programs that value diverse perspectives like yours more holistically. Your story is still unfolding, and with focused steps, you can rewrite the next chapter to one of triumph. Keep pushing—you've got the heart for this.
